questions.question: In pipe and tube examination, surface notches are used as reference reflectors instead of side-drilled holes when the concern is:
options: Mid-wall volumetric flaws
Surface-breaking flaws, because a notch gives a corner reflection like a surface crack at the ID or OD
Probe index-point verification
Screen horizontal linearity
questions.explanation: A notch breaks the surface and reflects from its corner exactly as a surface-breaking crack does, so ID/OD notches set sensitivity for the near-surface flaws that matter in pipe — a geometry no drilled hole reproduces.

questions.question: A natural crack returns less energy than a machined reflector of the same size chiefly because:
options: It is always smaller than the reference reflector
Tilt away from the beam, faceted rough surfaces that scatter sound, and tight closure that transmits part of the energy all reduce the returned amplitude
Machined reflectors corrode faster than cracks
The decibel scale compresses large signals
questions.explanation: Orientation, roughness, and partial transparency all rob the specular reflection a machined, air-backed, beam-facing target gives — the physical reason equivalent reflector size understates true flaw size.

questions.question: Neglecting attenuation, the far-field echo of a side-drilled hole falls by about how much per doubling of soundpath?
options: 3 dB
6 dB
9 dB
12 dB
questions.explanation: The cylindrical SDH sits between the plane back wall (about −6 dB per doubling) and the small disc reflector (about −12 dB): its echo falls roughly as 1/d^1.5, or about −9 dB per distance doubling.

questions.question: An echo drops from 80% FSH to 20% FSH after a repair grind. The amplitude change is:
options: −6 dB
−12 dB
−20 dB
−3 dB
questions.explanation: 20/80 = 0.25 and ΔdB = 20·log₁₀(0.25) ≈ −12 dB — each halving of amplitude is −6 dB, and this is two halvings.

questions.question: Time-corrected gain (TCG) differs from a screen-drawn DAC curve in that TCG:
options: Eliminates the need for calibration reflectors
Adds gain electronically as a function of time so equal reflectors read equal height at every depth — the correction flattened into a level line
Only works on analog instruments
Measures true flaw size directly
questions.explanation: TCG applies the same distance-amplitude information as a DAC, but as time-varying gain, so the reference response is a flat line across the range. It is still built from the same reference reflectors.

questions.question: UT finds a mid-weld indication with a rounded, swivel-tolerant signal; the radiograph shows a smooth, continuous dense line along the same length. The integrated evaluation should:
options: Treat the indication as slag and evaluate it under the volumetric amplitude-and-length clause, recording both methods' results
Average the UT and RT responses into one number
Reject it as a crack regardless of the evidence
Discard the RT result because UT found it first
questions.explanation: Both methods tell the same story — a volumetric slag line — so the volumetric acceptance clause applies, and the disposition record cites the results of both methods. Methods corroborate; they are never averaged.

questions.question: A mid-thickness lamination in rolled plate, unchanged since the fabrication baseline, carries low significance under static in-plane load primarily because:
options: Laminations always weld themselves shut in service
It lies parallel to the applied stress, so little stress acts across its faces — though it becomes serious if through-thickness tension is later introduced, as under a welded attachment
Ultrasound exaggerates lamination size
Plate codes do not address laminations
questions.explanation: A flat lamination under in-plane load sees almost no crack-opening stress. The appraisal flips when a nozzle or attachment weld pulls through the thickness — intended use and loading direction decide significance.

questions.question: During in-service inspection, which single finding most escalates an indication's evaluation?
options: Its amplitude is exactly at the recording threshold
It lies on the weld centerline
It was absent from the baseline and prior examinations — it appeared or grew in service
It is longer than it is deep
questions.explanation: Appearance or growth in service means an active mechanism — fatigue, corrosion, hydrogen, creep. Workmanship amplitude tables were written for stable fabrication conditions, not growing flaws, so the finding escalates regardless of amplitude.

questions.question: A 70° probe (sin 70° ≈ 0.94) peaks an indication at 40 mm soundpath in the first leg. Its surface distance from the index point is about:
questions.explanation: Surface distance = SP·sin θ = 40 × 0.94 ≈ 37.6 mm; depth would be SP·cos θ ≈ 13.7 mm. Mixing up sine and cosine puts the excavation in the wrong place.

questions.question: An indication is firmly characterized as a crack but peaks at only 45% of the reference level. Under typical construction codes the disposition is:
options: Accept — it is below the reference level
Accept if it is shorter than the thickness-based length limit
Reject — indications characterized as cracks are unacceptable regardless of amplitude or length
Downgrade it to porosity because the amplitude is low
questions.explanation: Characterization trumps amplitude: construction codes make cracks (and generally lack of fusion and incomplete penetration) rejectable at any amplitude. The amplitude tables govern only the other discontinuity classes.
